reply.proposal: Migrate packages/api from the current hand-rolled chain to Hono which provides a typed, onion-model middleware composition with explicit ordering via app.use(). Decompose the unwieldy logic into three isolated, testable middleware factories — authenticate using jose for JWT verification with a typed AppEnv context, rateLimit using rate-limiter-flexible backed by Redis via ioredis, and request logging using pino and pino-http with request-id propagation. Centralize ordering in a single createApp() factory that enforces the sequence request-id -> logger -> rate-limiter -> auth -> handler and introduces route grouping so public routes can skip auth while still getting logging/rate-limiting, plus Zod for request validation middleware. Roll out incrementally by wrapping existing handlers as Hono handlers behind a compatibility adapter, adding Vitest + supertest coverage for each middleware in isolation and for ordering guarantees, then deleting legacy middleware code once migrated.

Install and configure Redux Toolkit with react-redux as the global state layer for apps/dashboard. Create a centralized store at apps/dashboard/src/store/ using configureStore with Redux DevTools enabled and define domain-specific slices (e.g., user, filters, layout, widgets) via createSlice. Wrap the dashboard App root in <Provider> and replace prop-drilling and scattered useState/useContext logic with typed hooks useAppSelector and useAppDispatch. Migrate incrementally slice-by-slice, adding unit tests for reducers and selectors to prevent regression as features grow.

Initialize Prisma in packages/api by installing `prisma` as a dev dependency and `@prisma/client` as a dependency, then run `npx prisma init` to create `prisma/schema.prisma` configured for provider postgresql with DATABASE_URL from env. Define generator client and initial models ported from existing tables in the schema. Create a singleton wrapper at `src/lib/prisma.ts` using globalThis caching to avoid multiple PrismaClient instances in dev / hot-reload. Add package.json scripts for `db:generate`, `db:migrate`, and `db:studio`, hook generate into postinstall/build, and refactor existing raw SQL string queries to use the shared prisma client via service context for fully type-safe access.

We will create a dedicated typed BillingProviderClient in packages/api/src/clients/billing that encapsulates all REST calls behind domain methods (e.g., createInvoice, getSubscription). It will use Node 20's native fetch backed by an undici Pool/Agent for connection pooling and keep-alive. Retries will be handled via p-retry with exponential backoff + jitter, limited to 3 attempts and only for network errors, 408, 429 and 5xx, with Idempotency-Key headers enforced on all POSTs. Timeouts will be enforced with AbortSignal.timeout for a 2s connect and 10s total request timeout, and all responses will be validated with zod and mapped to typed errors like BillingTimeoutError, BillingRateLimitError, BillingUpstreamError with correlation IDs. Every call will emit structured logs via pino (with secret redaction) and OpenTelemetry traces/metrics for latency, retry count, and error code.

Do not expose the long-lived session token to JavaScript. Keep the session/refresh token in an HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Lax or Strict cookie that the browser sends automatically so it is never accessible via document.cookie. For API calls that need manual attachment, issue a short-lived 5-15 minute access token kept only in memory (JS closure/variable, not persisted) and send it via Authorization Bearer header. Obtain and renew the in-memory token via a /refresh endpoint that authenticates using the HttpOnly cookie and implements refresh-token rotation with reuse detection. This avoids persistent XSS-stealable storage while still allowing the SPA to attach tokens using fetch with credentials include.
acknowledged_rejections: Storing session or access tokens in localStorage
Storing session or access tokens in sessionStorage
Storing session token in non-HttpOnly JavaScript-readable cookie via document.cookie
Persisting long-lived session token in IndexedDB or Web Storage for manual attachment to fetch calls

reply.proposal: Add jest, ts-jest, and @types/jest as devDependencies in packages/api/package.json using the workspace package manager. Create a jest.config.js in packages/api with preset ts-jest, testEnvironment node, and roots set to src with moduleNameMapper matching the existing tsconfig paths. Add test scripts to packages/api package.json such as test running jest, test:watch running jest with watch flag, and test:coverage for coverage. Verify the setup by adding a sample unit test for a request handler that mocks req and res objects and ensures the test suite passes locally.

Use co-located CSS Modules for all new marketing pages under apps/dashboard. Each component will have its own ComponentName.module.css file in the same folder as ComponentName.tsx and import it directly. This gives us automatic component-scoped styles that satisfy the designer's preference for co-location with zero runtime cost, leveraging Next.js built-in CSS Modules support. We will use PostCSS for autoprefixing and CSS Variables for shared theming tokens from the design system.

Centralize the database schema as the source of truth using Drizzle ORM TypeScript definitions in a single db/schema.ts module. Configure drizzle-kit via drizzle.config.ts pointing to the schema file and to a drizzle/migrations output directory to auto-generate versioned, timestamped SQL by diffing the current code schema against its last snapshot. Integrate the migrator to run automatically on app startup and in CI/CD using drizzle-orm's migrate() function with the postgres driver. Update the developer workflow to edit schema.ts then run pnpm db:generate, review the generated SQL, and commit both the schema and migration files together.

Introduce a GraphQL endpoint as a facade in front of our existing domain services to replace coarse-grained REST for mobile. Mobile clients will request only the fields they need via typed GraphQL queries (e.g., `query { user { id name } }`) eliminating overfetching and enabling single-RTT screen loads for composed views. We will implement it using Apollo Server with a code-first schema in TypeScript and use DataLoader batching/caching to avoid N+1s against downstream services. To keep it production-safe we will add query complexity/depth limiting, persisted queries, and edge caching.

cells.context:
Project conventions and prior decisions:
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
- Use native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP — Node 18+ ships fetch; one 40-line wrapper gives retries, timeouts and JSON handling without a dependency
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
- Run unit tests with the built-in node:test runner — Zero test-framework dependencies; node --test runs TypeScript output directly and CI needs no transform config
- Style the dashboard with Tailwind utility classes — No runtime style computation, works with React Server Components, and design tokens live in one config
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
- Structured logging with pino, one JSON line per request — Fast, structured, and the ops dashboards parse JSON lines natively
- CI runs on GitHub Actions with a single reusable workflow — One workflow file, matrix per package; no CI vendor lock beyond what the repo host already implies
- pnpm workspace for the monorepo — Strict node_modules layout catches phantom dependencies; workspace protocol keeps internal packages linked
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier
- All API errors use the problem+json envelope — RFC 7807 gives clients one error shape; internal codes map to documented types
- Local development runs through docker compose — Postgres and the API start with one command; parity with the deploy image
- Prettier with the shared config; ESLint flat config for lint rules — Formatting is not reviewed; lint rules are; keeping the tools separate keeps both fast
- Feature flags read from environment variables, no flag service — A dozen flags do not justify a flag platform; env vars deploy atomically with the code
- OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat
- User file uploads go to S3-compatible object storage, never the database — Keeps the database small and backups fast; presigned URLs offload transfer
- Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth
- API is versioned in the URL path (/v1), breaking changes bump the version — Explicit and cache-friendly; header versioning confused two integration partners in the spike
- Each package keeps a README with run and test instructions — Onboarding doc lives next to the code it describes
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now

reply.proposal: Keep Hono and refactor to a single canonical middleware stack built in createApp() in packages/api: compose typed Hono middleware factories in explicit order so ergonomics are enforced by construction. Make the logger the outermost layer using the existing pino instance with request-id and OpenTelemetry context, followed by the existing token-bucket rate limiter backed by the Postgres UNLOGGED table, then auth that reads httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies / API keys and populates c.set('session'). Keep Zod at the route level via Hono's validator middleware to guarantee problem+json errors and reuse schemas for the OpenAPI spec, and add node:test integration tests that assert ordering and error shape.
acknowledged_rejections: Switching API framework to Express, Fastify, Koa or similar
Replacing REST/OpenAPI with GraphQL or tRPC
Introducing Redis for rate limiting or session caching
Replacing pino with Winston/Bunyan or other logger

reply.proposal: Do not introduce Redux Toolkit; keep the dashboard on Zustand per the existing architectural decision. Refactor the single store into isolated domain slices (session, filters, toasts, etc.) using Zustand's slices pattern with typed selectors and colocated actions to reduce coupling. Add zustand/middleware with immer for immutable updates and devtools for debugging, and enforce selector-based subscriptions to prevent over-renders. Add unit tests per slice with the existing node:test runner and document slice boundaries in the package README.
acknowledged_rejections: Redux Toolkit for global state in apps/dashboard

reply.proposal: Keep postgres.js with hand-written SQL per our data-access decision to retain visible query plans and no runtime abstraction layer. Add type-safety by enforcing strict TypeScript row interfaces and Zod schemas colocated with each query file, with typed row mappers as the single source of truth and a sql-tag lint check in CI. Continue using hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations so every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL and history stays linear. If we need stronger compile-time checks, evaluate a lightweight typed-SQL generator like pgtyped that still uses raw SQL, rather than an ORM.
acknowledged_rejections: Prisma ORM / Prisma Client for database access - rejected, violates decision to query Postgres with postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL with visible query plans
Prisma Migrate replacing hand-written SQL migrations - rejected, violates decision to use hand-written numbered forward-only SQL migrations

reply.proposal: Reuse the existing native fetch retry wrapper in packages/api for the billing provider integration, configured with provider base URL and API key from env vars. Set 3 retries with exponential backoff + jitter for 429/5xx, a 5s timeout via AbortController, and Zod schemas to validate billing provider responses at the boundary. reply.proposal: Continue storing session tokens only in httpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Lax cookies set by the Hono API — never expose the raw token to JavaScript. The SPA must not persist tokens in localStorage, sessionStorage, or Zustand; Zustand should hold only non-sensitive derived state like user profile and isAuthenticated. All API calls from both dashboard and mobile web use the shared native fetch wrapper configured with credentials: 'same-origin' (or 'include' for cross-origin) so the browser attaches the cookie automatically. This upholds our existing XSS mitigation and eliminates any client-side token handling while satisfying auth for fetch calls.
key_technologies: native fetch with credentials and retry wrapper
Zustand for non-sensitive session metadata only
Postgres UNLOGGED table for server-side session lookup
acknowledged_rejections: Storing session tokens in localStorage, sessionStorage, or in-memory JavaScript / Zustand
Making the session cookie readable from JavaScript so SPA can manually attach it via Authorization header
Client-managed Bearer token in fetch calls

Write request handler unit tests using Hono's app.request() test helper to invoke routes in-process, injecting mocks for Postgres and S3 at the handler factory boundary. Co-locate tests as *.test.ts next to handlers and expose them via pnpm test using node --test with tsx for TypeScript, updating the package README with run instructions. This preserves zero test-framework dependencies, no transform config in CI, and keeps test execution identical to production runtime.

reply.proposal: We should not implement auto-generated migrations from a schema definition. We deliberately chose hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations reviewed as literal SQL and querying Postgres via postgres.js to keep query plans visible and avoid a runtime abstraction layer. Switching to a DSL / ORM that generates migrations would hide SQL, make history non-linear, and directly violate those two conventions. Instead, improve ergonomics with a small pnpm script to scaffold numbered migration files and a CI step that runs them against the docker compose Postgres to catch syntax and drift errors. This preserves reviewability and keeps the stack at exactly one datastore with zero new dependencies.
acknowledged_rejections: Auto-generated migrations from schema definition via ORM tools like Prisma Migrate, Drizzle Kit, TypeORM
Replacing hand-written SQL and typed row mappers with a schema DSL / ORM abstraction layer

reply.proposal: Extend our existing REST endpoints with sparse fieldsets via a `fields` query param (e.g., GET /v1/users?fields=id,name) instead of introducing a new query language. Validate the param at the boundary with our existing Zod schemas against a per-resource allowlist and document it in the OpenAPI spec for discoverability. In the postgres.js layer, map the validated field list to a dynamic SELECT clause using typed row mappers so we actually reduce DB I/O and payload size, falling back to the full projection when the param is omitted for backwards compatibility. This preserves HTTP caching, URL path versioning (/v1), and our Hono + single Postgres stack while directly solving mobile overfetching.
acknowledged_rejections: GraphQL (including Apollo Server, graphql-yoga) - Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec; sparse fieldsets explicitly chosen to cover overfetching complaints

Project decision log:

  - REJECTED: Express — Larger dependency surface and slower cold starts; the middleware ecosystem is tempting but everything we need exists for Hono. Migration was evaluated and declined 2026-03

reply.proposal: Refactor the hand-rolled logic in packages/api into three typed Hono MiddlewareHandler modules — requestLogger that emits one pino JSON line per request, auth that reads httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies and validates against the Postgres UNLOGGED session table, and rateLimit that implements the existing token-bucket per API key. Centralize ordering in a single middleware.ts that registers app.use('*', logger) then app.use('/v1/*', rateLimiter) then app.use('/v1/*', auth) with explicit bypasses for /health and /v1/auth, using c.set/c.get to pass session and rate-limit state downstream. Delete the inconsistent wrapper functions and switch all routes to Hono's standard (c, next) composition, reusing existing Zod schemas at the boundary for validation. Cover each middleware with isolated node:test unit tests plus one integration test asserting order and problem+json error shape, and document the chain in packages/api README.

This retains the low-boilerplate, traceable approach that led to rejecting Redux Toolkit in the earlier spike, while directly fixing the organizational mess.

reply.proposal: We will not introduce Prisma in packages/api — it was rejected in the 2026-02 spike due to query-engine cold-start latency on serverless and hidden generated SQL, and Prisma Migrate conflicts with our hand-written migration policy. Instead we will achieve type-safe DB access within the existing postgres.js + hand-written SQL stack. We will add a small typed helper around postgres.js that pairs each SQL string with a colocated TypeScript row type and Zod schema for runtime row validation, keeping query plans fully visible. We will continue using numbered, forward-only SQL migrations and keep the stack at one datastore, which satisfies the type-safety goal without reintroducing the rejected runtime abstraction.

reply.proposal: Reuse the existing native fetch wrapper in packages/api for the billing provider calls. Configure timeouts via AbortSignal.timeout (with AbortController) and add exponential backoff with jitter for retryable 429/5xx responses, capped at 3 attempts and only for idempotent methods. Validate all responses with Zod schemas, emit a pino structured log line and an OpenTelemetry span on each attempt, and map final failures to our problem+json envelope as billing_provider_unavailable with retryable metadata.

This maintains the XSS protection validated in the 2026-04 pentest while still allowing the mobile web SPA to make authenticated API calls.

reply.proposal: Keep the current hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migration system. Every schema change continues to be authored and reviewed as literal SQL to keep prod and CI history linear and auditable. We will not introduce auto-generation from a schema definition because the 2026-03 incident showed generated diffs can silently include destructive changes like column drops that reached staging, and generated DDL is no longer trusted for schema changes. To address drift concerns without trusting generated DDL, add a CI check that builds the expected schema from applied migrations and diffs it against a checked-in schema reference, failing the build on unexpected divergence.

reply.proposal: Add sparse fieldsets to the existing REST API via a `fields` query param (e.g. `GET /v1/users?fields=id,name,avatar_url` and `fields[posts]=id,title` for compound responses) while keeping full responses as the default for backwards compatibility. Each endpoint defines an allowed-fields whitelist in its Zod schema which doubles as OpenAPI documentation, and the handler projects only those columns in the hand-written postgres.js SELECT and in the response serializer. This is cache-friendly (vary on query string) and adds no new runtime dependencies or datastores, directly addressing mobile overfetching within the REST/OpenAPI contract. Paginated collection endpoints can reuse the same param to keep payloads minimal on list screens.

reply.proposal: Reuse the existing native fetch with small retry wrapper pattern already standardized in packages/api for all outbound HTTP. Build a dedicated billing provider client at packages/api/src/clients/billing.ts that uses AbortController with AbortSignal.timeout for 10s request timeouts and parses JSON with explicit error handling. Configure retries with exponential backoff and jitter for network failures, 429 and 5xx responses, limited to 3 attempts and gated by idempotency keys for POSTs to prevent double-charges. Normalize all failures into a typed BillingProviderError containing status, provider request ID, retry count and sanitized response body to feed logging and alerting.
key_technologies: custom retry wrapper (existing 40-line wrapper in packages/api)

- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
  - REJECTED: Redux Toolkit — Boilerplate and indirection outweigh benefits at our state size; the team spent more time on slices than features in the spike
  - REJECTED: MobX — Implicit reactivity made renders hard to trace during the spike
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier

Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
  - REJECTED: auto-generated migrations — A generated diff silently included a column drop that reached staging in 2026-03 — rolled back; generated DDL is no longer trusted for schema changes
  - REJECTED: down migrations — False confidence — most destructive changes cannot actually be reversed; restore-from-backup is the real rollback
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
  - REJECTED: Redis — A second datastore to provision, monitor, back up and secure — ops budget decision is one datastore until scale forces otherwise (revisit at 10x traffic)
  - REJECTED: in-process memory cache — Breaks under multiple API instances; sticky sessions rejected as deploy constraint
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
  - REJECTED: Prisma — Query engine adds cold-start latency on serverless deploys and hides generated SQL; we hit both in the 2026-02 spike
  - REJECTED: TypeORM — Decorator-based entities fought our functional codebase style
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab

reply.proposal: We will not enable auto-generated migrations and will continue with hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations as the only schema change mechanism. Every change is authored as literal SQL and reviewed in PR to prevent silent destructive diffs like the column drop that reached staging in 2026-03. This keeps prod and CI history linear and keeps query plans visible alongside our existing postgres.js hand-written queries. No schema-definition-to-DDL tooling will be added to the pipeline. Rollback remains restore-from-backup, not down migrations.

reply.proposal: Keep the public API as REST with /v1 path versioning and OpenAPI spec — no protocol change. Add sparse fieldsets via a standardized query param like ?fields=id,displayName,avatarUrl validated against an allowlist per resource, with the server projecting only those columns at the DB/query layer. Document the param as a reusable OpenAPI component with examples, and include the fields value in the cache key and Vary logic to preserve HTTP caching. This lets mobile screens fetch exactly 2 fields per screen while retaining simple auth, cacheability, and multi-language tooling.
